meta.title: Lift state up vs keep state local in React
meta.summary: A filter drawer, results header, and product grid now need to reflect the same selection state. Would you lift state up or keep it local in each component?

scenario: You are building a React product page with three visible parts: a filter drawer, a results header, and a product grid. At first, the drawer owned its own selected category and sort order locally. Now the header must show the active filters, the grid must refetch when filters change, and a 'Clear all' action in the header should reset the drawer selections too. In the interview, the panel asks whether you would lift this state up to a common parent or keep it local inside each component and coordinate it another way.
prompt: Which direction would you defend for this prompt, and how would you explain when the other option is still the better answer?

options.label: Lift state up
options.summary: Works best when multiple sibling components need the same source of truth and must stay in sync.
whenItWins: Several sibling components need to read or update the same value, such as filters, open tabs, selected items, or sort order.
One component can trigger an action that should immediately affect the others, like a shared reset or clear-all button.
You want one obvious owner for the state instead of duplicating the same value across separate local components.
watchOutFor: Lifting too early can make a parent component carry UI details that only one child actually needs.
A weak answer lifts everything by default without checking whether the sharing problem is real or only hypothetical.

options.label: Keep state local
options.summary: Stays cleaner when the state is only relevant to one component and does not need to coordinate with siblings.
whenItWins: Only one component cares about the value, and no sibling needs to read or update it.
The state is a local UI detail such as hover state, an input draft, a temporary open panel, or a one-component toggle.
Keeping the state local preserves component ownership and avoids making the parent more complex than it needs to be.
watchOutFor: Once siblings must share the same value, separate local copies can drift out of sync or force awkward prop callbacks and effects.
A weak answer says local state is always simpler without explaining how the header, drawer, and grid will stay consistent.

decisionMatrix.title: Single-component UI detail
decisionMatrix.prompt: A value only affects one child and no other part of the page needs to read or change it.

cells.note: It can still work, but now the parent owns detail that did not need to leave the child.
This is the simpler answer because the state belongs to one component and can stay there.

decisionMatrix.title: The exact prompt in this battle
decisionMatrix.prompt: The drawer, header, and grid must all reflect the same filters, and the header can reset the drawer state.

cells.note: This is easier to defend because the state is now shared page state, not just a local drawer detail.
You can force it, but coordinating copies across siblings quickly becomes harder to explain and maintain.

decisionMatrix.title: Shared filters plus local UI details
decisionMatrix.prompt: The actual filters are shared, but some drawer-only details like a temporary search draft or open accordion sections still matter only inside the drawer.

cells.note: Good for the shared filter state, but weaker if you also lift every tiny drawer interaction that no sibling uses.
Still correct for drawer-only state, which is why a strong answer often combines both patterns with a clean ownership boundary.

evaluationDimensions.title: Who really owns this state?
evaluationDimensions.description: The first question is whether the value belongs to one child or has become shared page state used by multiple parts of the UI.

evaluationDimensions.title: Do sibling components need to stay in sync?
evaluationDimensions.description: If two or three siblings must always agree on the same value, one shared owner usually becomes easier to defend.

evaluationDimensions.title: How many places can update the value?
evaluationDimensions.description: The more places that can change it, the more valuable one source of truth becomes.

evaluationDimensions.title: Will lifting make the parent too heavy?
evaluationDimensions.description: A strong answer also protects the parent from becoming a dumping ground for tiny UI details that should have stayed local.

evaluationDimensions.title: Can you separate shared state from local UI state?
evaluationDimensions.description: Many real solutions are mixed: lift the shared value, keep purely local interaction details close to the child.

strongAnswer.title: A strong answer here would lean lifting state up because the filter state is now shared across siblings
strongAnswer.summary: This prompt no longer describes drawer-only state. The header, drawer, and grid all need the same filters, and more than one component can trigger updates.
reasoning: If the selected category only affected the drawer UI itself, I would keep it local because that is clearer and lighter.
In this prompt, the same filter state now drives the header display, the grid behavior, and the clear-all action. That means there should be one shared owner above those siblings.
I would still keep drawer-only details local, such as temporary open sections or tiny interaction state, so the parent only owns what is truly shared.
strongAnswer.recommendation: For this exact prompt, I would lift the shared filter and sort state to the nearest common parent of the drawer, header, and grid, but keep purely local drawer interaction details inside the drawer component.

interviewerPushback.question: Why not keep the state local and just notify the other components?
interviewerPushback.answer: Because then the drawer still owns a value that the header and grid also depend on. Once several siblings need the same source of truth and more than one place can update it, lifting the shared state usually gives a cleaner model than coordinating copies.

interviewerPushback.question: Should all state always be lifted if multiple components exist?
interviewerPushback.answer: No. Only the state that is truly shared should move up. Local UI details that matter to one child should stay local so the parent does not become overloaded.

interviewerPushback.question: What would change your answer?
interviewerPushback.answer: If the filter value stopped affecting siblings and became a drawer-only concern again, I would move back toward local state because the extra parent ownership would no longer be earning its cost.

answerExamples.title: Weak answer
answerExamples.answer: I would lift the state because that is what React says to do.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: It does not. This sounds like a slogan, not an explanation of ownership, sibling sync, or when local state is still the better choice.

answerExamples.title: Decent answer
answerExamples.answer: I would probably lift the filters because several components need them now.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This is directionally fine, but it still needs a stronger explanation of shared ownership and the boundary between shared filter state and drawer-only local state.

answerExamples.title: Strong answer
answerExamples.answer: For this prompt I would lift the shared filter and sort state to the nearest common parent because the drawer, header, and grid all need the same values and more than one component can trigger updates like clear-all. If a piece of state is only a drawer interaction detail, I would keep that local so the parent owns only what is truly shared.
answerExamples.whyItWorks: This sounds strong because it explains the real ownership change, shows why siblings need one source of truth, and still protects local UI details from being lifted without need.

answerFramework: Start by asking whether the state belongs to one child or is now shared across siblings.
Say who should own the value for this prompt.
Explain which components need to read or update it and why one source of truth matters.
Show where the other option is still correct, especially for local UI details.
End with a clear boundary, for example: lift shared page state, keep one-component interaction state local.
antiPatterns: Lifting every piece of state to the parent just because multiple components exist on the page.
Keeping separate local copies of a value that several siblings must treat as one shared truth.
Ignoring the difference between shared business state and tiny UI interaction state.
Answering with a React slogan instead of explaining ownership and synchronization.
